title: The ASNT NDT Level III Certification Program
narrationScript: Everything we have studied so far keeps certification inside the employer's walls. This lesson steps outside them. The ASNT NDT Level Three certification program is a central, third-party program run by the national society, and for most working Level Threes it is the credential that anchors a career. First, the scope and the idea. In a central certification program, an independent body, not the employer, examines candidates against a published body of knowledge and issues a certificate in its own name. The society's Level Three program is the oldest and most widely recognized example in NDT, and the standard we studied last lesson leans on it directly, requiring the central certificate as the foundation for employer Level Three certification. The society also operates a broader central certification program covering other levels, but the Level Three program is the one the Basic examination expects you to know in detail. A few definitions keep the rest of the lesson clean. The candidate is the person applying to be examined. The certificate documents that the holder passed the program's examinations in a method, shown as an endorsement for that method. The basic examination covers the common core, certification programs, materials and processes, and a survey of the major methods, exactly the ground this course covers. The method examination goes deep into one method at Level Three depth, principles, techniques, procedures, and the codes that govern application. Now be precise about the outcome, because this is the most commonly misunderstood point in the whole module. Passing the examinations earns a certificate that attests to knowledge of the published body of knowledge. It is powerful evidence of qualification. It is not, by itself, authority to work. Authority comes from the employer, who certifies and designates responsibilities under its own written practice. A consultant with a wallet full of central certificates still cannot sign a procedure for a company until that company makes that consultant its Level Three. Qualification evidence is central. Authority is local. Say that twice. Eligibility for examination is built on education and experience that trade against each other. A candidate holding a four-year degree in engineering or science from an accredited institution needs at least one year of experience in NDT comparable to Level Two work. A candidate with two years of engineering or science study at a university, college, or technical school needs at least two years of comparable experience. A high school graduate needs at least four years. The program's published documents state the current criteria, and the pattern to remember is simple. More formal technical education means less required experience, and it is never zero. The qualification examinations come as a pair. Every candidate passes the basic examination once, and then one method examination for each endorsement sought. The examinations are multiple choice, several hours long, administered under controlled and proctored conditions, and graded against a passing score set by the program, historically in the neighborhood of seventy percent. Results are reported as pass or fail. A candidate who fails may examine again after a waiting period, and the program limits how many attempts can be made before additional waiting or evidence of further preparation is required, so candidates should prepare to pass rather than to sample the test. Pass the basic examination and a method examination, and the society issues the certificate with that method endorsement. Add methods later by passing additional method examinations. The basic examination does not have to be repeated while certification remains in force. Validity runs five years from issue. Recertification at the five-year mark takes one of two routes. The first is sitting the examinations again. The second, where the program allows, is renewal by documented professional credits, points earned through continuing work in the field, teaching, professional society activity, publications, and continuing education. Either way, the burden is on the certificate holder to show the knowledge is alive. For the examination you are preparing for, anchor these facts. Central certification provides examination-based, portable evidence of qualification that travels with the person across employers. Eligibility trades education against experience, one, two, or four years. Basic plus method examinations produce a certificate with method endorsements, valid for five years, renewable by reexamination or by credits. And the certificate informs, but never replaces, the employer's own act of certification. Hold that picture together with the previous three lessons and module one is complete. You now know who qualifies people, who certifies them, on whose evidence, under which documents, and for how long.
imagePrompt: A mid-career NDT engineer studies at a wooden desk stacked with method handbooks, handwritten notes, and a scientific calculator in a quiet industrial training center library in warm evening light.

sections.heading: Central Certification in an Employer-Based World
sections.body:
The two documents you have studied so far keep certification **inside the employer's walls**. The ASNT NDT Level III certification program moves one piece of it outside: a **central certification** program in which an independent body — the national society — examines candidates against a published body of knowledge and issues a certificate in **its own name**. The program's scope is Level III qualification evidence across the NDT methods, and it interlocks with the employer-based documents: written practices under SNT-TC-1A commonly **accept** the ASNT certificate in lieu of in-house Level III examinations, and CP-189 **requires** it as the basis for employer Level III certification. ASNT also operates broader central certification covering other levels (the ASNT Central Certification Program), but the Level III program is the one the Basic examination expects in detail.

Definitions worth fixing now: the **candidate** is the person applying for examination; the **certificate** is the society's documentation that its examinations were passed; a **method endorsement** identifies each method in which the holder qualified; the **Basic examination** covers the common core (certification programs, materials and processes, and a survey of the methods — precisely this course's ground); and a **Method examination** tests one method at Level III depth: principles, techniques, procedure and specification knowledge, and applicable codes.

sections.heading: What the Certificate Does — and Does Not Do
sections.body:
Be precise about the **certification outcome**, because it is the most commonly misunderstood point in this module. Passing the program's examinations earns a certificate attesting that the holder **demonstrated knowledge of the published Level III body of knowledge**. That is powerful, portable, third-party **evidence of qualification** — it travels with the person from employer to employer and does not evaporate at resignation the way employer-based certification does.

What it is **not** is authority. The certificate does not, by itself, authorize anyone to sign procedures, designate techniques, or accept hardware for a company. Authority flows from the **employer's own act of certification** under its written practice: the employer reviews the evidence (the ASNT certificate among it), adds any required employer-specific examination, and designates the person as *its* Level III with defined responsibilities. A consultant holding endorsements in five methods still cannot stamp a procedure for a fabricator until that fabricator certifies and designates them. The clean formulation: **qualification evidence is central; authority is local.**

sections.caption: Three eligibility pathways trade formal education against required NDT experience comparable to Level II work.

sections.heading: Eligibility: Education Trades Against Experience
sections.body:
Before sitting the examinations, a candidate must meet the program's **eligibility criteria**, built on the principle that formal technical education and field experience trade against each other — but experience never drops to zero. The program's published model:

- **Four-year degree** in engineering or science from an accredited institution → at least **one year** of NDT experience comparable to Level II work
- **Two years** of engineering or science study at a university, college, or technical school → at least **two years** of comparable experience
- **High-school graduate** (or equivalent) → at least **four years** of comparable experience

Two qualifiers carry weight. *Comparable to Level II* means real method work — performing, interpreting, evaluating — not adjacent paperwork; candidates document it, and the program may verify. And the criteria are the **program's published requirements**, stated in its current documents — a Level III advising a candidate checks the current edition rather than folklore. Candidates already certified Level II typically clear the experience bar with their documented method time; engineers entering from design or metallurgy typically clear education easily and must build the method experience.

sections.caption: Eligibility pathways under the program's published model.
headers: Minimum NDT experience (comparable to Level II)
rows: Two years of engineering or science study (university, college, or technical school)

sections.heading: The Qualification Examinations
sections.body:
Qualification rests on two examinations, and the division of labor between them organizes your entire study plan.

- **Basic examination** — taken **once**. It covers the common core every Level III needs regardless of method: personnel qualification and certification programs (SNT-TC-1A, CP-189, and this central program), materials, fabrication, and product technology, and a working survey of the major NDT methods. This course maps to that examination.
- **Method examination** — one per **endorsement** sought: the chosen method at Level III depth, including principles and variables, techniques and their selection, procedure preparation and qualification, and the codes, standards, and specifications that govern application.

Both are **multiple-choice**, several hours long, delivered under controlled, proctored conditions (computer-based delivery is the norm), with the passing score set by the program — historically in the neighborhood of **70 percent**. The questions are drawn against the published **topical outlines**, which is why studying to the outline, rather than to a favorite textbook's table of contents, is the high-percentage strategy.

sections.caption: From examinations to a living certificate: Basic plus Method examinations yield endorsements valid five years, renewed by reexamination or documented professional credits.

sections.title: Evidence is central; authority is local
sections.body: The ASNT Level III certificate is **portable, examination-based evidence of qualification**. The employer's certification — under its own written practice — is what **assigns authority** to write procedures, designate techniques, and accept results. Level III exam questions love to test whether you keep these two acts separate.

sections.heading: Results, Certification, Validity, Recertification
sections.body:
**Examination results** are reported as **pass or fail** against the program's passing score. A candidate who fails may reexamine after a **waiting period**, and the program limits the number of attempts within an eligibility window before requiring additional waiting or evidence of further preparation — so the professional approach is to prepare to pass, not to scout the test.

**Certification.** Passing the Basic examination plus a Method examination yields the certificate with that **method endorsement**. Additional endorsements come from additional Method examinations; the Basic examination is **not repeated** while certification remains in force.

**Validity** runs **five years** from issue.

**Recertification** at the five-year mark takes one of two routes: **reexamination**, or — where the program provides it — renewal by **documented professional credits**: points accumulated through continued NDT employment and practice, teaching and examining, professional society participation, publications, and continuing education. The credit route exists because a working Level III demonstrably exercising the body of knowledge is maintaining it; the documentation burden sits on the certificate holder, and falling short of the credit threshold sends the holder back to the examination room. Either way, an expired certificate means requalifying through examination — which is strong motivation to track the five-year date.

figures.title: Eligibility pathways to the Level III examinations

checkQuestions.question: Qualification under the ASNT NDT Level III program consists of passing:
options: A general, a specific, and a practical examination administered by the employer
The Basic examination once, plus a Method examination for each endorsement sought
A single combined examination covering all NDT methods
An interview panel plus a portfolio review
checkQuestions.explanation: The program pairs one Basic examination (certification programs, materials and processes, method survey) with a Method examination at Level III depth for each method endorsement; the Basic is not repeated while certification remains in force.

checkQuestions.question: A candidate completed two years of engineering study at a technical college. Under the program's published model, the minimum NDT experience required for examination eligibility is:
options: One year comparable to Level II work
Two years comparable to Level II work
Four years comparable to Level II work
No experience, since education substitutes completely
checkQuestions.explanation: Education trades against experience but never to zero: a four-year degree pairs with one year, two years of study with two years, and a high-school graduate with four years of Level II-comparable experience.

checkQuestions.question: An ASNT NDT Level III certificate is valid for:
options: Three years from issue
Five years from issue
Ten years from issue
Life, once earned
checkQuestions.explanation: Validity runs five years, after which the holder recertifies by reexamination or, where provided, by documented professional credits.

checkQuestions.question: A consultant holds current ASNT Level III certificates in UT and RT. Before she can approve procedures for a pressure-vessel fabricator, the fabricator must:
options: Do nothing — the ASNT certificates already grant that authority anywhere
Certify and designate her as its Level III under its own written practice, including any employer-specific examination it requires
Send her certificates to ASNT for revalidation
Have her repeat the Basic examination for the fabricator's industry sector
checkQuestions.explanation: The central certificate is portable qualification evidence; authority to act for an employer comes only from that employer's certification and designation under its written practice.

checkQuestions.question: At the five-year mark, a busy practicing Level III who would rather not retest can maintain certification by:
options: Submitting documented professional credits (continued practice, teaching, society activity, continuing education) where the program provides that route
Simply continuing to work — active employment renews it automatically
Having his employer extend the certificate under its written practice
Paying the renewal fee with no further evidence
checkQuestions.explanation: Recertification takes one of two routes: reexamination, or a documented professional-credits renewal where the program allows it. Employment alone, employer action, or payment without evidence cannot renew a central certificate.

references: CP-105-2024 Basic Topical Outline §1.3–1.3.9 (NDT Level III certification program)
ASNT NDT Level III program documents (eligibility, examinations, validity, recertification — summarized, current edition)
ANSI/ASNT CP-189 (Level III certification basis, contrast reference, summarized)
